feature_name: C64x+ VLIW core and instruction set
description: The C64x+ fixed-point DSP core issues up to eight instructions per cycle using a VLIW pipeline. It supports saturation arithmetic, bit manipulation, and SIMD-style operations on 16-bit lanes for high throughput. A Harvard architecture with instruction/data paths and prefetch reduces stalls under load. L1P/L1D caches and configurable L2 memory improve locality and latency to external memory. Deterministic real-time behavior depends on scheduling, cache planning, and disciplined memory access patterns.
evidence: CPU/ISA Reference (SPRU732); Device Overview (SPRS371)
dependencies: None; optional cache/L2 configuration during early init
Remarks: Use TI calling conventions and consider pipeline hazards; stage hot code/data; validate WCET with caches configured as for production.

description: The on-chip L1P cache (~32 KB) reduces instruction fetch latency for hot paths. It is transparent but benefits from function layout and tight-loop locality to raise hit rates. L2 can be configured as SRAM to lock critical code segments when determinism is required. Aligning sections and avoiding pathological conflict patterns helps stability. Profile and verify instruction footprint under representative workloads.
evidence: SPRU732: Cache Architecture (L1P); SPRS371: Memory Subsystem
dependencies: Optional cache configuration during startup
Remarks: Keep critical loops contiguous; place ISRs and boot code deterministically; avoid self-modifying code and ensure I-cache coherency on updates.

description: The L1D cache (~32 KB) accelerates access to frequently used data structures. Software should favor spatial and temporal locality, with alignment and padding to cache-line boundaries. DMA activity must be coordinated to prevent stale data or write-back hazards. L2 configuration complements L1D to balance working set capacity versus determinism. Use explicit cache maintenance around DMA buffers that are cacheable.
evidence: SPRU732: Cache Architecture (L1D); SPRS371: Memory Subsystem
dependencies: Cache maintenance or non-cacheable mapping for DMA buffers
Remarks: Use writeback/invalidate sequences at DMA boundaries; segregate MMIO as non-cacheable; audit shared buffers for alignment and size.

description: The L2 memory can operate as SRAM, cache, or a mix to trade determinism for capacity. Locking code/data in L2 SRAM eliminates replacement effects for time-critical routines. As a cache, L2 extends the hot working set at lower latency than external DDR2. The chosen partition should be fixed across builds and coordinated with linker scripts. Establish memory attributes to align with the L2 role and intended DMA usage.
evidence: SPRU732: L2/Cache Configuration; SPRS371: Memory Map
dependencies: Early boot L2 partitioning and attribute setup
Remarks: Reserve L2 regions for ISRs, stacks, and descriptors; document partition in build artifacts; verify latency under interrupt and DMA load.

description: IDMA performs deterministic on-chip moves between L2 and L1 under CPU control. It is suited for short, latency-sensitive staging of code or data without EDMA setup overhead. The engine is limited to internal address spaces and complements EDMA for bulk transfers. Cache settings must be considered to maintain coherency. Use when bounded, small transfers are required in tight control loops.
evidence: SPRU732: IDMA Chapter
dependencies: None; coordinate with cache settings
Remarks: Prefer IDMA for small staged copies; use EDMA for large or peripheral-driven moves; avoid starving instruction fetch and data pipelines.

description: The DDR2 controller provides JEDEC-compliant access to external SDRAM. Initialization programs SDCR, SDTIMx, and SDRCR and issues the standard power-up and mode register sequence. Board-specific drive strength, termination, and any VTP/PHY settings must be applied. Pattern testing should validate timing margins before enabling caches and high-throughput DMA. Ongoing refresh is handled by SDRCR per the configured interval.
evidence: DDR2 Controller User Guide: SDCR/SDTIM1/SDTIM2/SDRCR; Initialization Sequence
dependencies: PSC enable; stable PLL/clock; board DDR parameters
Remarks: Derive timing strictly from the memory datasheet; verify with walking patterns and stride mixes; enable DMA only after successful validation.

description: EDMA3 moves data among memories and peripherals with low CPU overhead. PaRAM sets describe 1D/2D/3D transfers with link and chain for scatter-gather. Channels map to queues and transfer controllers, with completion and error signaling via IPR/ICR and CCERR. QDMA supports CPU-triggered copies for short bursts. Proper alignment of descriptors and buffers improves cache behavior and throughput.
evidence: EDMA3 UG (C645x): PaRAM OPT/SRC/DST/A-B-C CNT; DCHMAP; IPR/ICR; CCERR
dependencies: PSC enable for EDMA CC/TC; INTC mapping; event routing
Remarks: Align descriptors to recommended boundaries; clear completion in ICR after reading IPR; size bursts to cache lines to avoid thrashing.

description: The EMAC provides 10/100/1000-Mbps Ethernet with DMA-based packet queues. An MDIO controller discovers and configures external PHYs and monitors link state. Address and VLAN classification are supported via host-configured tables where applicable. Statistics counters aid bring-up and monitoring. Descriptor rings must reside in coherent memory with proper alignment.
evidence: EMAC/MDIO: MDIO_CONTROL/MDIO_USERACCESS; CPDMA descriptor interface
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; MDIO clock; external PHY; DMA rings; INTC
Remarks: Initialize MDIO and confirm link before enabling RX/TX; configure MAC address and VLAN policy; enforce cache coherency on descriptors and buffers.

description: The PCI block supports host-master and target transactions with a conventional PCI host. Software sets up the configuration header, BARs, and command/status to expose DSP windows. Interrupts and EDMA cooperate to achieve high throughput for bulk data. Proper reset/negotiation are required at bring-up. Address translation and endianness must match the host view to avoid data corruption.
evidence: PCI User Guide: Config Header, BARs, Command/Status; Reset/Bring-up
dependencies: PSC enable; host reset sequencing; BAR/window mapping; EDMA for bulk
Remarks: Validate BAR mappings and byte ordering with the host; throttle EDMA to bus limits; confirm posted-write behavior and ordering.

description: UHPI enables an external host to access the DSP memory map directly. HPIC controls bus width, reset, and handshake while HPIA/HPID form the address/data path. The interface supports polled and interrupt-driven operation to balance latency and complexity. It simplifies host-assisted boot and diagnostics. Arbitration should avoid contention with CPU and EDMA masters for shared regions.
evidence: UHPI: HPIC, HPIA/HPIAW, HPID; Usage Notes
dependencies: PSC enable; host timing/handshake; INTC if used
Remarks: Program width and reset in HPIC before enabling; validate timing with the host; partition shared memory to prevent races.

description: VLYNQ provides a point-to-point serial link that maps peer resources into the local address space. Configuration defines lane rate, framing, and address windows on both ends. Link status and error registers support robust operation and recovery. Memory-mapped semantics allow simple loads/stores and DMA across the link. Address windows must be aligned and consistent between peers.
evidence: VLYNQ: Link Control/Status; Address Window Registers
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; peer configuration; address window mapping
Remarks: Bring up with polled read/write tests before enabling DMA; confirm window alignment and permissions; monitor error/status during stress.

description: McBSP supports TDM and I2S-like formats with programmable clocks and frame sync via SRGR. RCR/XCR define word length and slots; DRR/DXR provide data paths with optional FIFO behavior. SPCR and PCR control state machines and pin polarity. Typical use cases include audio codecs and telecom framers. EDMA commonly services RX/TX for low interrupt overhead.
evidence: McBSP: SPCR, RCR, XCR, SRGR, PCR, DRR/DXR
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA setup; INTC mapping
Remarks: Sequence resets and state enable per user guide; verify clocking and frame timing; align EDMA bursts to frame boundaries.

description: TSIP provides multi-link telecom interfaces with programmable framing and bit clocks. Separate TX/RX links support time-slot based transfers across TDM networks. FIFOs and status registers enable low-latency servicing via EDMA or interrupts. Integration eases attachment to framers and baseband devices. Correct pinmux and timing are essential for reliable operation.
evidence: TSIP User Guide: Control/Status and FIFO Registers
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA channels; INTC mapping
Remarks: Validate framing and line rates against the framer; use EDMA to meet slot deadlines; monitor underrun/overrun flags in bring-up.

description: The SPI module supports master/slave transfers with programmable CPOL/CPHA and word length. SPIFMT and SPIDELAY configure bit timing and inter-transfer delays to match slaves. SPIDAT/SPIBUF form the data path with optional interrupt signaling. EDMA can offload long transfers to reduce CPU load. Chip-select polarity and timing must match board wiring.
evidence: SPI: SPIGCR0/1, SPIFMTx, SPIDELAY, SPIDAT, SPIBUF
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; CS wiring; optional EDMA/interrupts
Remarks: Reset and configure GCR/FMT before enabling; match CPOL/CPHA and bit order; align buffers and consider EDMA for sustained bursts.

description: The I2C module implements master/slave roles with standard and fast modes. ICPSC and ICCL/H set prescale and SCL timing while ICMDR controls transfers and ICSTR reports status. External pull-ups are mandatory for SDA/SCL integrity. Interrupts simplify multi-byte and error handling flows. Arbitration and NACK conditions must be handled explicitly in software.
evidence: I2C: ICOAR, ICSAR, ICPSC, ICCLK(L/H), ICCNT, ICMDR, ICSTR
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; external pull-ups; INTC mapping
Remarks: Handle NACK and arbitration lost per ICSTR; program ICCNT correctly; assert STOP only after the final ACK, and clear status bits in order.

description: The UART is 16550-like with RX/TX FIFOs and configurable word length, parity, and stop bits. DLL/DLH divisors generate baud from the input clock, with PWREMU_MGMT controlling low-power gating. Interrupt or EDMA servicing can be used depending on throughput. FIFO reset/trigger settings affect latency and jitter. Accurate clocking is required to keep baud error within tolerance.
evidence: UART: THR/RBR, LCR, FCR, DLL/DLH, IER/IIR, PWREMU_MGMT
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; reference clock; INTC mapping
Remarks: Reset FIFOs before enabling; validate baud error; pick interrupt or EDMA strategy based on sustained rate; document line settings.

description: GPIO exposes up to 32 pins with per-bit direction and data control. Writes use SET_DATA/CLR_DATA while reads use IN_DATA for current state. Edge-detect supports rising/falling edges with latched status and banked enables for interrupts. Debounce is not provided in hardware and must be external if needed. GPIO can source EDMA sync events in certain integrations.
evidence: GPIO: DIR, SET/CLR_DATA, IN_DATA, SET/CLR_RIS_TRIG, BINTEN, INTSTAT01
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; INTC mapping; optional EDMA sync
Remarks: Enable via PSC before access; clear edge flags; document and filter noisy inputs; validate interrupt routing and polarity.

description: The 64-bit timer can split into two 32-bit timers for independent operation. PRD registers set the period for periodic or one-shot modes while TCR/TGCR control run-state and prescalers. Compare/overflow can raise interrupts for scheduling and timestamps. Capture/PWM modes are available on certain pins via pinmux. Accurate prescaler computation is essential to meet timing targets.
evidence: Timer: TIM12/TIM34, PRD12/PRD34, TCR, TGCR, INTCTL
dependencies: PSC enable; INTC mapping; optional PINMUX for capture/PWM
Remarks: Choose 64-bit or dual-32 mode early; compute prescalers from the known input clock; keep ISR short and defer noncritical work.

description: The PLL multiplies the reference to generate core and system clocks while PLLDIVx derive peripheral clocks. Reprogramming requires a defined lock/unlock and GO command sequence with status polling. Clock stability must be ensured before enabling DDR2 and high-speed peripherals. Changing PLL rates affects downstream timing and may require coordinated divider updates. Follow documented sequences to avoid lock loss and bus faults.
evidence: PLLC: PLLCTL, PLLM, PLLDIVx, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT
dependencies: Stable reference clock; correct programming order; PSC sequencing
Remarks: Poll PLLSTAT during transitions; update dividers coherently; validate derived clocks under load and temperature.

description: PSC manages power and clock gating for modules grouped in domains. Each peripheral’s LPSC must transition to ENABLE before register access to avoid bus stalls. Transitions are requested via PTCMD and observed through PTSTAT and MDSTAT. Proper sequencing reduces power and prevents access faults. Disable unused modules once the system stabilizes to save energy.
evidence: PSC: PTCMD, PTSTAT, MDCTL[n], MDSTAT[n]
dependencies: Required by all peripheral drivers; domain/state sequencing
Remarks: Always enable via PSC before touching registers; poll for completion; gate idle modules; record sequencing in bring-up scripts.

description: Many pins are multiplexed among peripheral functions and GPIO. KICK0/KICK1 must be unlocked with device-specific keys before writing protected PINMUX registers. Correct mux selection ensures signal availability and avoids conflicts. Boot configuration may set initial mux states that software should revisit. Reserved bits must be preserved using read-modify-write sequences.
evidence: Device Configuration: KICK0/KICK1 keys; PINMUXx
dependencies: KICK unlock sequence; ordered with PSC to avoid unsafe toggling
Remarks: Plan a single, early pinmux step; document conflicts; relock after configuration; keep a board-specific mux table under revision control.

description: The C64x+ interrupt model maps many system events into the limited CPU interrupt lines via INTMUX. An event combiner aggregates peripheral events to reduce vector pressure. ISRs must clear the peripheral source before acknowledging the controller to prevent retrigger. Proper vector table setup and prolog/epilog discipline minimize latency. EDMA completion and error events integrate cleanly into this framework.
evidence: SPRU732: Interrupts/Event Combiner; SPRS371: INTMUX
dependencies: Peripheral enables; INTMUX mapping; optional event combiner
Remarks: Centralize vector table and mapping; clear source first; keep ISR minimal and defer work to tasks; document priorities.

description: The device boots from sources such as I2C, SPI, UART, PCI, or UHPI selected by strap pins. The ROM performs minimal setup then loads a first-stage image to internal memory. Application code typically reprograms PLL and enables required PSC modules. Capabilities vary by ROM revision noted in the device manual. Unified board initialization should follow boot to ensure deterministic setup.
evidence: SPRS371: Reset/Boot Configuration
dependencies: Board strap pins; ROM revision; external boot media
Remarks: Keep first-stage minimal and robust; hand off to a common init that programs PLL/PSC/DDR; validate boot timings and handoff.

description: The IDLE instruction gates the CPU clock until an enabled interrupt occurs. PSC can further gate module or domain clocks for deeper power savings. Wake sources must remain active and routed through the interrupt fabric. Entry/exit must preserve data integrity and time constraints. Proper use reduces thermal load and average power in steady state.
evidence: SPRU732: IDLE; SPRS371: PSC Domain Gating
dependencies: Wake interrupt routing; PSC domain configuration
Remarks: Prefer IDLE to polling in wait loops; verify wake interrupts are unmasked; quiesce DMA before gating domains; measure wake latency.

description: JTAG enables halt, trace, and real-time debug capabilities for development. Some peripherals include emulation-aware controls to keep clocks during halt for safe inspection. Debug sessions can perturb timers, DMA, and links if not coordinated. Boot timing and emulator attach order may influence system behavior. Follow emulation notes to minimize interference with real-time operation.
evidence: SPRU732: Emulation; SPRS371: Emulation Notes
dependencies: External emulator; toolchain integration
Remarks: Avoid halting during active transfers; enable emulation-aware options; script attach/reset; document known interactions with peripherals.

description: Peripherals expose status and error flags typically cleared by writing one to the bit. EDMA reports completion and errors via IPR/ICR and CCERR to support robust flow control. Drivers should log and clear these conditions promptly in ISRs or polling paths. A unified error-handling scheme simplifies diagnostics across modules. Counters and timestamps help identify recurring faults.
evidence: EDMA3: IPR/ICR/CCERR; Peripheral status/clear registers
dependencies: Per-driver ISR/polling; logging infrastructure
Remarks: Standardize error codes and logs; follow write-1-to-clear semantics; include rate limiting and health metrics.

description: Public documentation does not describe dedicated security or cryptographic accelerators for this device. Secure boot or hardware access control is not covered in standard manuals. If present, features may be silicon- or system-specific and not generally available. Software should assume no hardware crypto or secure boot by default. Reevaluate if new collateral or revisions introduce such capabilities.
evidence: UNKNOWN
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: Use software cryptography if required; rely on system-level protection; review future silicon or NDA documents for changes.

description: MAR/MPAX settings control cacheability and address extension for external regions. Correct attributes ensure DMA buffers and MMIO windows have intended behavior with caches. These settings complement L2 partitioning to balance performance and determinism. Misconfiguration can cause stale data, write combining issues, or bus faults. Establish and freeze attribute tables early in the boot process.
evidence: SPRU732: MAR/MPAX Configuration
dependencies: Privileged CPU configuration coordinated with cache and DMA usage
Remarks: Mark MMIO non-cacheable; align DMA buffers to cache lines; document attribute maps and verify with targeted cacheability tests.}
